coredata.dc:description: Understanding the cytotoxicity of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) is crucial for their applications, and various biochemical assays have been used to study the effects of CDs on cells. Knowledge on the effects of CDs from a biophysical perspective is integral to the recognition of their cytotoxicity, however the related information is very limited. Here, we report that atomic force microscopy (AFM) can be used as an effective tool for studying the effects of CDs on cells from the biophysical perspective. We achieve this by integrating AFM-based nanomechanics with AFM-based imaging. We demonstrate the performance of this method by measuring the influence of CDs on living human neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells at the single-cell level. We find that high-dose CDs can mechanically induce elevated normalized hysteresis (energy dissipation during the cell deformation) and structurally impair actin skeleton. The nanomechanical change highly correlates with the alteration of actin filaments, indicating that CDs-induced changes in SH-SY5Y cells are revealed in-depth from the AFM-based biophysical aspect. We validate the reliability of the biophysical observations using conventional biological methods including cell viability test, fluorescent microscopy, and western blot assay. Our work contributes new and significant information on the cytotoxicity of CDs from the biophysical perspective.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of the AFM-based biophysical study of CDs-induced actin skeleton alteration. CDs were synthesized by the hydrothermal method (see Materials and methods and Fig. S1 in the Supplementary Materials), and used to treat the SH-SY5Y neural cells. Afterwards, force profiles of the cells were measured in incomplete cell culture media by AFM nanoindentation, from which key mechanical parameters were extracted. Meanwhile, the cells were imaged by AFM in incomplete cell culture media to obtain their high-definition morphological images. The AFM biophysical (mechanical and imaging) results show that high-dose CDs were detrimental to the actin skeleton of SH-SY5Y cells, which were validated by biochemical approaches including fluorescence imaging and WB assay.

Characterization of CDs. (a) TEM image of CDs. (b) High-resolution TEM image of a single CD. (c) Size distribution histogram of CDs, fitted with the Gaussian function (n = 800 from three-independent experiments). (d) Digital photos of CDs solutions: (i) CDs solution with a concentration of 10 mg/mL in Milli-Q water; (ii) The solution shown (i) diluted 30 folds in Milli-Q water; (iii) The solution in (ii) under a 365 nm UV lamp. (e) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs in Milli-Q water (1 mg/mL) with the peak value (360 nm) marked. (f) Emission spectra of CDs obtained at the excitation wavelength of 420–500 nm with 10 nm increase.

Viability and morphology of SH-SY5Y cells treated by CDs for 6 h. (a) Viability of cells treated by CDs of various concentrations. (b-d) Optical images of cells treated by 0 (control), 50 and 75 μg/mL of CDs for 6 h, respectively. The error bar represents the SD. * and ** denote p < 0.05 and p < 0.01, respectively.

AFM Nanomechanical analysis of SH-SY5Y cells treated by varying concentrations of CDs for 6 h. (a) Optical image showing alignment of an AFM tip over the central region of a cell subjected to an AFM nanomechanical measurement. (b) An example force-distance curve acquired on the bottom surface of a Petri dish in the incomplete cell culture media, in which the approach (black) and retraction (blue) curves were well-overlaid and the zero point indicates the contact point where the AFM tip physically touched the bottom surface of the Petri dish. (c) An example force-distance curve for an SH-SY5Y cell. The zero point (contact point) is the location where the cell began to be deformed during the compression process while the negative distance in the approach curve indicates the indentation deformation of the cell. Once the AFM tip contacted the cell, a positive loading force led to the cell compression. The negative force in the retraction curve implies the adherence of the tip to the cell surface during the tip retraction. Two key parameters were extracted by considering both the approach and retraction curves, including the normalized hysteresis (Ψ) and the adhesion work (A w). Ψ is calculated by A 1/(A 1 + A 2), where A 1 and A 2 are the green and yellow areas, respectively. The adhesion work (A w) is calculated by the integration of the purple area. (d-f) Ψ and (g-i) A w for the cells treated by CDs of different concentrations [0 (control), 50, and 75 μg/mL]. The data were fitted to the Lorentz function with fitted values presented as mean ± SD.

AFM imaging analysis of the morphology of SH-SY5Y cells treated by varying concentrations of CDs for 6 h. (a-c) Typical deflection images of cells treated with 0 (control), 50, and 75 μg/mL of CDs. (d-f) The corresponding height images of (a-c). (g-i) High-resolution images correspond to the regions marked by green squares in (d-f).

Fluorescence images of SH-SY5Y cells treated with CDs of various concentrations for 6 h. (a-c) The CDs concentrations are 0 (control), 50 μg/mL, and 75 μg/mL, respectively. Actin filaments are in green while the cell nuclei are in blue.

WB assay of proteins of SH-SY5Y cells upon exposure to CDs for 6 h. The protein amounts of F-actin, cofilin, and IL-6 were measured for the cells without CDs treatment or with the treatment by either 50 μg/mL or 75 μg/mL CDs. GAPDH served as the housekeeping protein.

head.ce:title: Nanoscale detection of carbon dots-induced changes in actin skeleton of neural cells

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Understanding the cytotoxicity of fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) is crucial for their applications, and various biochemical assays have been used to study the effects of CDs on cells. Knowledge on the effects of CDs from a biophysical perspective is integral to the recognition of their cytotoxicity, however the related information is very limited. Here, we report that atomic force microscopy (AFM) can be used as an effective tool for studying the effects of CDs on cells from the biophysical perspective. We achieve this by integrating AFM-based nanomechanics with AFM-based imaging. We demonstrate the performance of this method by measuring the influence of CDs on living human neuroblastoma (SH-SY5Y) cells at the single-cell level. We find that high-dose CDs can mechanically induce elevated normalized hysteresis (energy dissipation during the cell deformation) and structurally impair actin skeleton. The nanomechanical change highly correlates with the alteration of actin filaments, indicating that CDs-induced changes in SH-SY5Y cells are revealed in-depth from the AFM-based biophysical aspect. We validate the reliability of the biophysical observations using conventional biological methods including cell viability test, fluorescent microscopy, and western blot assay. Our work contributes new and significant information on the cytotoxicity of CDs from the biophysical perspective.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a new type of carbon-based zero-dimensional material, carbon dots (CDs) have great potential applications in biosensing, drug delivery and bioimaging due to their favorable properties such as strong fluorescence, high-water solubility, and robust chemical stability [1–5]. It is crucial to assess the toxicity of CDs before their biological-related applications. In the past years, the effects of CDs on cells have been intensively investigated with various biochemical approaches [6–10]. These pioneering investigations found that CDs could affect cell viability [11], metabolism [12], cell cycle [13], gene expression [14], etc. Although these studies have disclosed important information on the cytotoxicity of CDs from the biochemical perspective, they are largely failed to reveal the cytotoxicity of CDs from the perspective of biophysics. Cell biophysical characteristics (e.g., nanomechanical and morphological properties) are pertinent to cell functions and behaviors such as migration, growth, differentiation, aging, and signaling [15–17]. Therefore, probing the effects of CDs on cells from the biophysical aspect is integral to comprehensively comprehending the cytotoxicity of CDs, however, hitherto the related work has been rarely reported.

Atomic force microscopy (AFM) is a powerful label-free and high-resolution tool [18–23], which allows measuring the nanomechanics and morphology of various biological samples spanning from tissues [24], cells [25–28] to biomolecules [29–33] in different environments. Previously, AFM has been employed to investigate the interactions between cells and nanomaterials [34–36]. In these studies, alterations in the Young’s moduli of cells were normally analyzed from AFM measured nanomechanical data with the Hertz model, and/or the changes in cellular appearances were observed by AFM imaging. Based on the measured nanomechanical and/or morphological changes, the cytotoxicity of nanomaterials could be evaluated to a certain degree [37,38]. Despite such progress, it has been scarcely shown that AFM could be utilized alone to provide an in-depth evaluation of the cytotoxicity of nanomaterials from the biophysical perspective. More often than not, AFM needs to be coupled with other techniques to reveal the cytotoxicity of nanomaterials, hindering the broad application of AFM in biophysical research areas.

Herein, we report that AFM can be used alone to reliably disclose the cytotoxicity of nanomaterials from the biophysical aspect by simultaneously exploiting its sensitive nanomechanical measurement and high-definition imaging functions, and the reliability of this biophysical strategy is validated by using biochemical methods. We demonstrate the performance of this strategy by investigating the effects of CDs on living SH-SY5Y human neuroblastoma cells at the single-cell level (Fig. 1 ). We extract a key mechanical parameter, namely normalized hysteresis (energy dissipation during the cell deformation) of SH-SY5Y cells from AFM force measurement, and collect high-definition AFM morphological images of the cells. The AFM images clearly show that high-dose CDs make damages to the cellular actin skeleton (actin filaments), which is highly consistent with the mechanically measured increased normalized hysteresis. The alteration of the actin skeleton induced by high-dose CDs is confirmed by fluorescent microscopy and western blot (WB) assay, and is in good agreement with the cell viability tested by a Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8). These biological studies corroborate the effectiveness of using AFM alone to reveal in-depth information on the effects/cytotoxicity of CDs against SH-SY5Y cells from the biophysical perspective. This work not only enhances our understanding of the cytotoxicity of CDs on neural cells, but also proves that the integration of AFM nanomechanics with AFM imaging is a useful biophysical tool for probing cell-nanomaterials interactions.

Polyethylenimine (PEI, 50 % solution, v/v), glucose (analytical grade), and ethylene glycol (analytical grade) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich (Shanghai, China). CDs were solvothermaly synthesized in water-glycol medium by using glucose as carbon source, and modified with PEI according to a previous reported method [39]. First, 3 g of glucose was added to 150 mL solution of v: v/1:1 glycol and Milli-Q water (18.2 MΩ·cm), which was subjected to ultrasound until the solution became clear. Then, the mixture was transferred to a 250 mL round-bottomed flask in oil bath, followed by heating at 180 °C to reflux for 2.75 h while the mixture was magnetically stirred to allow the formation bare CDs. Next, 4 mL of 20 % (v/v) PEI solution was mixed with 100 mL of the bare CDs solution and stirred for 3 h at 80 °C to make the CDs modified by PEI. Finally, the formation of the CDs was confirmed when the color of the solution turned to brown under naked eyes and strong cyan fluorescence became visible under an ultraviolet lamp. The synthesized CDs were further dialyzed in Milli-Q water using a dialysis bag (JincloneBiotechnologyInc.,Beijing,China) with a cutoff molecular weight of 3500 Da for 24 h, and stored in the dark at 4 °C for later use. The concentration of the CDs solution was determined by the following steps: (1) put 1 mL solution into an empty tube with a known mass; (2) the solution was freeze-dried, and the tube containing CDs was weighed; (3) the mass of the dry CDs was calculated; (4) the concentration was determined according to the solution volume and the dry mass of the CDs.

The size and morphology of the CDs were imaged using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM, Tecnai G2 F20, FEI, Hillsboro, USA) operating at an accelerating voltage of 200 kV. Fluorescence spectra of CDs solution were recorded using an F-7000 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi Ltd., Tokyo, Japan) while the ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectra were obtained using a UV-2450 UV–Vis spectrometer (Shimadzu Co., Kyoto, Japan).

Cell culture media, phosphate buffer solution (PBS, pH 7.4) and trypsin-EDTA were purchased from Gibco, Life Technology (Shanghai, China). The cell culture media were composed of a 1:1 mixture (v/v) of Dulbecco’s Modified Eagle’s Medium (DMEM) and Ham’s/F-12 Nutrient Medium (F-12) supplemented with 10 % fetal bovine serum and 1 % penicillin–streptomycin. CCK-8 was ordered from Beyotime Biotechnology Inc. (Shanghai, China). The 1:1 (v: v) mixture of DMEM and F-12 is termed as “incomplete cell culture media”, which was used in the cell viability assays and AFM experiments. Consumable utensils such as tissue flasks, Petri dish, tubes and microplates were purchased from Corning Inc. (New York, USA). SH-SY5Y cells (Beijing 4A Biotech Co., Ltd., Beijing, China) were cultured in tissue culture flasks in a 5 % CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 °C according to the procedures described in literature [40]. The culture media were changed every other day until the cells were ∼ 80 % confluent. The cells were collected and resuspended in the cell culture media to a density of 2 × 105 cells/mL for use in later experiments. The growth conditions of cells were monitored with an optical microscope (IX53, Olympus Co., Tokyo, Japan).

SH-SY5Y cells prepared as described in the section of “2.2. Cell culture” was plated in a polystyrene 96-well microplate (100 μL/well) to grow in the incubator for 24 h. Aliquots of CDs stock solution described in the section of “2.1. CDs synthesis and characterization” were diluted in the cell culture media to a series of different concentrations (100, 75, 50, 25, 10, 7.5, 5 and 2.5 μg/mL) before the cell viability tests. Next, the culture media in each well were replaced either with the same volume of CDs solutions of various concentrations (experimental group) or with the same volume of cell culture media (control), and then the cells were incubated for different periods (3, 6, 12, 24 and 48 h) in the incubator before rinsed once with the incomplete cell culture media to remove residual CDs. The viability of SH-SY5Y cells was assessed using the CCK-8 assay by following the provider’s instructions. In brief, 100 μL incomplete cell culture media containing 10 μL CCK-8 solution was added to each well of a microplate and incubated for 1.5 h at 37 °C. Afterwards, the microplate was measured by a microplate reader (Infinite M200pro, Tecan Group Ltd., Männedorf, Switzerland), and the optical density value at 450 nm (OD450) was recorded and used to determine the cell viability [41]. The cell viability of experimental groups was normalized to that of control correspondingly and expressed in percentage [42]. Cells in the microplate were also observed with an optical microscope. Three replicates were run per sample in each of three independent experiments, and the value for each treatment represents the averaged value.

SH-SY5Y cells (5 mL) prepared as described in the section of “2.2. Cell culture” were seeded in a Petri dish (60 mm × 15 mm) and incubated for 24 h in a 5 % CO2 humidified atmosphere at 37 °C. Afterwards, the media were removed, and the Petri dish was rinsed with 2 mL of PBS before adding 4 mL of either CDs solutions of various concentrations (50 and 75 μg/mL, experimental group) or with the same volume of cell culture media (control). Next, the cells were incubated for a further 6 h before the culture media were removed from the Petri dish. Finally, the Petri dish was gently rinsed with PBS for three times and added into 5 mL of incomplete cell culture media. The prepared sample was used immediately for either AFM imaging or force measurement, and each experiment was completed within ∼ 1 h to minimize the cell state change. SH-SY5Y cells could adhere stably to the bottom surface of the Petri dish during the AFM measurement, so there was no need to modify the bottom surface of the Petri dish or to fix the cells with chemicals.

Cell samples were measured using the Dimension Edge AFM (Bruker Co., Santa Barbara, USA) in the incomplete cell culture media at room temperature. Soft AFM cantilevers (MLCT, Bruker Co., Camarillo, USA) having a nominal spring constant of 0.01 N·m−1 were used. For AFM imaging, the instrument was operated in contact mode at a minimized load and an optimized scan rate of 0.3 Hz/line. AFM images were analyzed with the NanoScope Analysis software (3.0) included in the instrument. The force measurement was performed essentially by following the protocol in literature [43]: (1) Each cantilever employed was calibrated against the bottom surface of the Petri dish of a cell sample in the incomplete cell culture media to obtain the cantilever sensitivity and spring constant under the aid of AFM software; (2) the AFM probe was positioned on the soma apex of an individual living cell with the help of an optical microscope coupled to the AFM system to circumvent the possible artifact caused by the side-on interactions between the cell and AFM probe due to their geometries; (3) Force curves were collected at a loading/unloading rate of 1 μm/s to minimize the stiffening effects attributed to the hydrodynamic drag force and the underneath hard substrate (bottom surface of the Petri dish). The threshold loading force was set at 2 nN to prevent damage to the cell membrane; (4) The collected raw force curves were transformed into force-distance curves by removing cantilever bending using a home-developed code based on Igor Pro (Version 6.04, Wavemetrics Inc., Lake Oswego, USA), which were further analyzed by using self-written Matlab procedures (Version R2010a, Math-Works Inc., Natick, USA). At least 300 raw force curves were collected for either the control group or the experimental group by measuring a minimum of 10 force curves on each of the more than 30 cells from five independent experiments. The extracted data were plotted and fitted to the Lorentz functions using the Origin software (Origin Lab Co., Northampton, USA).

The cells were treated by CDs solutions (50 and 75 μg/mL) or the cell culture media (control) for 6 h before they were stained in dark room with standard staining protocols [43]. Chemicals used in the staining process were purchased from Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (Shanghai, China), which were diluted in PBS when it was necessary. Briefly, the cells were fixed in 4 % paraformaldehyde for 15 min at 25 °C, permeabilized with 0.1 % Triton X-100 for 20 min at 25 °C, and then the Triton X-100 was blocked by 1 mL 1 % bovine serum albumin (BSA) for 3 h at 37 °C. To stain actin filaments, the cells were incubated in 200 µL Alexa FluorTM 488-conjugated phalloidin solution (1:20) for 40 min at ∼ 25 °C. Microtubules and intermediate filaments were also stained, respectively (see the Supplementary Materials). Following this, the nuclei were further stained by incubating the cells in 200 µL DAPI solution (1: 1800) for 5 min at ∼ 25 °C to stain the DNA. Afterwards, the samples were carefully washed twice with PBS between any two different operation steps. Finally, the cells were submerged in 2 mL PBS and imaged with a confocal microscope (LSM900, Carl Zeiss Microscopy GmbH, Jena, Germany).

Primary antibodies were ordered from Abcam (Shanghai, China) while the secondary antibodies, lysis buffer, tris-buffered saline-Tween 20 (TBST), and an electro-chemiluminescence kit were purchased from Shanghai Wellbio Technology Co. (Shanghai, China). Cells treated with CDs solutions of different concentrations (50 and 75 μg/mL) or cell culture media (control) for 6 h were retrieved for each sample and lysed by Radio Immunoprecipitation Assay lysis buffer for 15 min in an ice bath. The solution for each sample was transferred to tubes and centrifuged at 12,000 × g for 5 min. Next, the supernatant in the tubes was applied onto a 10 % SDS-PAGE gel for electrophoresis. Afterwards, the protein was transferred to a polyvinylidene fluoride filter membrane (Bio-Rad Laboratories Inc., Hercules, USA) and blocked with 5 % BSA at room temperature for 2 h before the membrane was washed three times with TBST. The antibodies were diluted in TBST when it was necessary. The membrane was incubated with primary antibodies of cofilin (1:500 dilution), F-actin (1:1000 dilution) and IL-6 (1:1000 dilution) overnight at 4 °C before washed with TBST three times, followed by further incubating for 2 h at room temperature with horseradish peroxidase-conjugated secondary antibodies (1:2000 dilution) which were stained using the electro-chemiluminescence kit for 5 min at room temperature. Finally, the protein bands were photographed by a chemiluminescence image analysis system (Tanon 4600, Shanghai Tanon Science & Technology Co., Ltd., Shanghai, China). Glyceraldehyde-3-phosphate dehydrogenase (GAPDH) from each of the cell samples was used as a loading control in the analysis.

All data were presented as mean ± SD. A one-way analysis of variance with Tukey’s test included in Origin Software was used to analyze the statistical significance (p-value), and p < 0.05 was considered statistically significant.

We characterized the morphology of synthesized CDs by TEM. It can be seen that the CDs were mono-dispersed particles and relatively uniform size (Fig. 2 a). The high-resolution TEM image of a typical particle (Fig. 2b) shows that the particle exhibited a nearly spherical shape with typical lattice spacing of graphitic carbon. The particles having a diameter between 3 and 4 nm account for ∼ 63 % while those having a diameter between 4 and 5 nm account for ∼ 29 %. The statistics indicate that the diameter of ∼ 92 % of the particles ranges from 3 to 5 nm and centers around 3.81 ± 0.26 nm by applying the Gaussian fitting to the data (Fig. 2c). X-ray diffraction spectra indicate that the CDs were quite pure (see Fig. S2 in the Supplementary Materials). Besides the morphology, we also evaluated the fluorescence emission performance of the CDs (Fig. 2d). The CDs solution with a concentration of 10 mg/mL presented a brown color [Fig. 2d (i)], but turned into faint yellow when the solution was diluted 30 folds [Fig. 2d (ii)]. The diluted CDs solution emitted strong fluorescence [Fig. 2d (iii)] when excited at 365 nm by a UV lamp. The UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CDs solution (1 mg/mL) presents a pronounced peak at 360 nm (Fig. 2e). From the emission spectra of CDs under different excitation wavelengths from 420 to 500 nm, it was observed that the fluorescence intensity gradually decreased with the increase in the excitation wavelength (Fig. 2f), implying the excitation-dependent emission character of the CDs. These observations manifest that the CDs have been successfully synthesized, which are in good agreement with literature [39].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effects of CDs on cell viability and morphology
ce:section.ce:para: Next, we evaluated the effects of CDs on the viability of SH-SY5Y cells using a CCK-8 assay. Fig. 3 a shows the viability of SH-SY5Y cells upon exposure to CDs of various concentrations [from 0 (control) to 100 μg/mL] for 6 h. As can be seen, the viability of SH-SY5Y cells changed insignificantly until the concentration reached 75 μg/mL. Besides, we also measured the viability of cells exposed to CDs of various concentrations for a series of different durations, i.e., 3, 12, 24, and 48 h (see Fig. S3 in the Supplementary Materials) and found that the longer the exposure duration the lower the CDs concentration required to decrease the cell viability with significance. The results indicate that the influence of CDs on SH-SY5Y cells is both dose and time dependent. Since the cell viability is normally related to the cellular morphology, we observed the morphology of SH-SY5Y cells upon exposure to different CDs doses [from 0 (control) to 100 μg/mL] for 6 h by using an optical microscope (Fig. 3b-d, and Fig. S4 in the Supplementary Materials). There was no observable change in the cell appearance between the cells without suffering from exposure (Fig. 3b) and those exposed to CDs with a concentration lower than 50 μg/mL (Fig. 3c). However, many cells changed evidently from a normal irregular shape to an abnormal oval shape when the cells treated by a higher CDs dose, e.g., 75 μg/mL (Fig. 3d) or 100 μg/mL (see Fig. S4 in the Supplementary Materials). It has been reported that CDs could be high efficiently internalized/endocytosed by neural cells [44]. Our observed phenomena are in good agreement with the observations in the viability and morphology changes of mouse fibroblasts treated by the same type of CDs with a similar size and dosage [13].

ce:section.ce:section-title: AFM nanomechanics of SH-SY5Y cells treated with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: We investigated the effects of CDs on the mechanical properties of SH-SY5Y cells by employing an AFM-based nanoindentation technique. The measurement was carried out in the incomplete cell culture media under the aid of an optical microscope (Fig. 4 a), and the sample condition was monitored by the optical microscope. A typical force-distance curve measured on the bottom surface of the Petri dish of a cell sample is shown in Fig. 4b, in which the approach and retraction curves are perfectly overlaid before the AFM probe contacted the bottom surface of the Petri dish, indicating that the hydrodynamic drag effect is negligible and that the AFM has a high precision in force measurements [45]. There is no observable deformation in the approach curve of the force-distance curve, confirming the bottom surface of the Petri dish is rigid enough for the calibration of AFM cantilevers as well as for the support of cells in the AFM force measurement (Fig. 4b). A typical force-distance curve collected from the soma apex of a cell is shown in Fig. 4c, which is distinct from that measured on the bottom surface of the Petri dish (Fig. 4b). The force-distance curve of a single cell presents three pronounced features when compared with that measured on the bottom surface of the Petri dish: (1) the indentation deformation (negative distance) increased nonlinearly with the applied load; (2) evident hysteresis between the approach curve and retraction curve appeared (green area in Fig. 4c); (3) obvious adhesion (purple area in Fig. 4c) was observed in the retraction curve. These observations manifest the different properties of the cells from the substrate.

Normally, the Hertz model was used in AFM-based nanomechanics to extract the apparent Young’s moduli of cells [46,47]. The Hertz model is originally developed for characterizing the indentation behavior of an isotropic, linear elastic, and homogeneous object by an indenter of simple shape. However, cells are viscoelastic material with a highly complex and heterogeneous structure, therefore the mechanics of cells is unlikely to be well-described by the Hertz model [48]. Herein we adopted another two apparent mechanical parameters to describe the cell mechanics, i.e., normalized hysteresis (Ψ), and adhesion work (A w) (Fig. 4c). These two mechanical parameters are physically meaningful and related to the cell biological structure. Ψ implies the viscoelasticity of the cell ascribing to its soft glassy rheology and structural heterogeneity while A w reflects the adhesive property between the AFM probe and cell surface in the unloading process. A w was measured by the integration of the area (purple) between the negative force part of the retraction curve and the zero-force line. Ψ was calculated according to [49,50]
(1)
ψ =
A 1
A 1
+
A 2
where A 1 represents the area (green) between the approach and retraction force curves above the zero-force line while A 2 represents the area (yellow) of the retraction force curve above the zero-force line. A 1 is the energy dissipation during the indentation while A 1 + A 2 denotes the energy input during the indentation.

With these two parameters, we analyzed SH-SY5Y cells exposed to different concentrations of CDs (0, 50, and 75 μg/mL, respectively). The statistical histograms for Ψ and A w are shown in Fig. 4d-f and Fig. 4g-i, respectively. The value of Ψ for the cells without suffering from CDs (0.50 ± 0.01, Fig. 4d) is comparable to that of the cells treated by 50 μg/mL of CDs (0.55 ± 0.01, Fig. 4e). However, the value of Ψ for the cells after exposure to 75 μg/mL of CDs (0.64 ± 0.01, Fig. 4f) is significantly higher than those of the former two groups. In contrast, the values of A w present a different trend when compared to Ψ. Although the value of A w for the cells without the treatment by CDs (1.72 ± 0.13 × 10-17 N·m, Fig. 4g) is comparable to that of the cells treated by 50 μg/mL of CDs (1.79 ± 0.13 × 10-17 N·m, Fig. 4h), which is significantly higher than that of the cells subjected to the treatment by 75 μg/mL of CDs (1.45 ± 0.14 × 10-17 N·m, Fig. 4i). The results of both the two mechanical parameters indicate that the mechanical properties of the cells treated by 75 μg/mL of CDs are different from those of the cells without exposure to CDs or with exposure to 50 μg/mL of CDs, which are highly consistent with the results of the cell viability and the optically observed cell morphology (Fig. 3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: AFM imaging of SH-SY5Y cells treated with CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Besides measuring the nanomechanics, we also examined the morphological alteration of SH-SY5Y cells using AFM by taking advantage of its high spatial resolution. Both the deflection and height images were collected (Fig. 5 ). It was found that the cells without exposure to CDs (Fig. 5a and d) had a similar elongated polygonal shape to that of the cells treated with 50 μg/mL of CDs (Fig. 5b and e). However, upon the treatment with 75 μg/mL of CDs, the cells changed from the elongated polygonal/irregular shape to a rounded shape accompanying with the disappearance of dendrite-like structures (Fig. 5c and f), they are in excellent agreement with what observed using the optical microscope (Fig. 3b-d). Similar phenomena were previously reported for U87 cells treated by high-dose nicotine [51]. Interestingly, filamentous structures were observed on the cell surface, which can be assigned to the actin stress fibers underneath cell plasma membrane, according to previously AFM imaging studies [52–54]. Previous studies showed that AFM could image actin filaments through the cell plasma membrane without apparent damage to the cell [55]. Remarkably, we found from the AFM deflection images that the actin filaments of the cells without exposure to CDs (Fig. 5a) or treated with 50 μg/mL of CDs (Fig. 5b) were long, compact, and well-aligned whereas those for the cells treated with 75 μg/mL of CDs were shorter, more dispersed, and disorganized (Fig. 5c). Although these phenomena cannot be readily observed from the corresponding AFM height images (Fig. 5d-f), which can be easily seen from the AFM height images with a smaller scan area (Fig. 5g-i). Filamentous structures of the cells without exposure to CDs are arranged as thick fibers (Fig. 5g) while the fibers become less prominent for the cells treated by 50 μg/mL of CDs (Fig. 5h). For the cells exposure to the high-dose CDs (75 μg/mL), the fibers are obviously thinner, disorganized and fragmented (Fig. 5i) when compared with those of the cells without suffering from the CDs. The AFM imaging results suggest that the high-dose CDs (75 μg/mL) induced reduction and fragmentation of actin filaments in the SH-SY5Y cells, consistent with a recent study that zeolitic imidazolate framework-8 nanoparticles led to fragmented or disappeared actin filaments in the human aortic endothelial cells [56].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of actin changes of SH-SY5Y cells treated with CDs by fluorescence microscopy
ce:section.ce:para: We also investigated the changes of SH-SY5Y cells treated with CDs by confocal fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 6 ). From the fluorescence images, the arrangements of actin filaments (green) and nuclei (blue) can be clearly observed. It was found that obvious actin loss occurred in the SH-SY5Y cells treated with the high-dose CDs (75 μg/mL, Fig. 6c) and that the actin filaments surrounding the nucleus (blue) became less abundant and more disorganized when compared with the cells without the treatment by CDs (Fig. 6a) or with the treatment by low-dose CDs (50 μg/mL, Fig. 6b). Meanwhile, the distribution of actin filaments also indicates that some the SH-SY5Y cells treated with the high-dose CDs (Fig. 6c) changed to a rounded shape with much less dendrite-like structures. These observations are highly consistent with what observed by AFM imaging (Fig. 5). Of note, the nuclei of the cells treated by all the CDs doses mentioned above show no observable changes and always maintain an oval shape, which are consistent with the results that the viability of the cells treated by 50 μg/mL CDs had no statistically significant change while that of the cells treated by 75 μg/mL CDs only declined ∼ 12 %.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Validation of actin changes of SH-SY5Y cells treated with CDs by WB assay
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the molecular mechanism of cell changes induced by CDs exposure, WB assay was performed to analyze the protein levels, in which the amount of GAPDH was used as the loading control [57]. The change of a tested protein can be evaluated by normalizing its amount (integrated density of the band) to the amount of GAPDH in the same lane. From a typical example (Fig. 7 ), it can be seen that the amount of F-actin is evidently decreased while the amounts of cofilin and interleukin-6 (IL-6) are increased for the cells exposed to the high-dose CDs (75 μg/mL). There are no significant changes in the amounts of these proteins for the cells subjected to the treatment by 50 μg/mL CDs. Since the amount of F-actin is directly proportional to the abundance of actin filaments, the result of WB analysis complies with the observed reduction of actin filaments by AFM imaging (Fig. 5). Previous evidence showed that overexpression of cofilin caused actin destabilization and reduced actin prevalence [58,59], so the WB analysis of cofilin further supports the disorganization and reduction of actin filaments revealed by AFM imaging (Fig. 5). In addition, the amount increase of IL-6 indicates less expression of adhesion molecules such as intercellular cell adhesion molecule-1 (ICAM-1) and vascular cell adhesion molecule-1 (VCAM-1) on the glial cell surface [60,61], which may result in decreased cell adhesion.

ce:section.ce:section-title: AFM-based biophysical study of CDs-induced actin changes in SH-SY5Y cells
ce:section.ce:para: As shown above, we employed AFM to investigate the mechanics (Fig. 4) and morphology (Fig. 5) of SH-SY5Y cells treated by CDs. On one hand, we mechanically measured the increase of Ψ of the cells treated by the high-dose CDs (75 μg/mL), which means that the cells became less recoverable or less elastic from the indentation and that more energy has been dissipated during the loading/unloading cycle [41,62]. On the other hand, we visually observed the reduction and disorganization of actin filaments for the cells suffered from the high-dose CDs (75 μg/mL), which were also validated by the fluorescence microscopy (Fig. 6) and WB assay (Fig. 7). Both the mechanical and structural changes are also in accordance with the cell viability (Fig. 2a). Previous studies have demonstrated that the disruption of actin filaments led to the decrease of the apparent Young’s moduli/elasticity of cells [63–65]. Therefore, the results of our AFM mechanical and imaging measurements are highly consistent with each other, manifesting that we could reliably detect CDs-induced actin changes/ cytotoxicity in SH-SY5Y cells with an in-depth AFM-based biophysical approach including both cellular mechanics and imaging. With the fluorescence microscopy, we also investigated the other two cytoskeletal components (see Fig. S5 in the Supplementary Materials). No observable changes were found in the microtubules of the SH-SY5Y cells before and after the treatment by the CDs. Although a slight reduction in the intermediate filaments was observed in the SH-SY5Y cells treated by the high-dose CDs (75 μg/mL), this should not compromise the conclusion drawn above. The mechanically observed decrease of A w of the cells treated by the high-dose CDs (75 μg/mL) is likely caused by the deduction of adhesion molecules on the cell surface, which is supported by the overexpression of IL-6 (Fig. 7) and previous reports that IL-6 inhibited the expression of adhesion molecules (e.g., ICAM-1 and VCAM-1) of neural cells [60,61].

In summary, we investigated the interaction between CDs and SH-SY5Y cells, and showed that the effects of CDs on the cells could be detected in-depth with an AFM-based biophysical strategy, namely, the integration of AFM nanomechanical measurement with AFM high-definition imaging. We found that high-dose CDs (75 μg/mL) exerted cytotoxicity on SH-SY5Y cells, as evidenced by mechanically measured elevated normalized hysteresis and visually observed actin filament reduction and fragmentation. The change in the mechanical parameter correlates well with the actin skeleton alteration. The cytotoxicity revealed by AFM-based biophysical strategy was validated by using conventional biological approaches including the CCK-8 test for cell viability, fluorescence microscopy for actin skeleton, and WB assay for molecular changes related to actin alterations. The results indicate that useful and in-depth information on the action of CDs on neural cells can be reliably disclosed by using the demonstrated AFM-based biophysical approach. Collectively, the present work sheds new insights into the effects of CDs on cells, and demonstrates a useful tool for the evaluation of biosafety of nanomaterials and chemicals, which could also find potential applications in drug screening areas.
